findings.law: FTC Act §5 (15 U.S.C. §45 — net-impression deception); CCPA §1798.140(v) (display name + device ID = PI)
evidence: docs/legal/privacy-policy-ORIGINAL.md:21 (no-name framing) vs :63 (display names are personal data)
supabase/migrations/0001_init.sql:113-123 (display_name + device_id persisted together)
findings.recommended_fix: Reconcile the framing: 'No account or email is required to play. You may enter a display name, which is shown to others and to the host and is stored with a device identifier we set on your browser.' Remove the unqualified 'we do not ask for your name.'

findings.title: Document header references a corrected published policy and an app/privacy page; neither exists, and there is no live privacy page
findings.policy_quote: the corrected, published policy lives in docs/legal/privacy-policy.md + app/privacy/page.tsx.
findings.problem: The header asserts a corrected, published policy exists at docs/legal/privacy-policy.md and app/privacy/page.tsx, but neither file exists — docs/legal/ contains only this ORIGINAL baseline, and there is no app/privacy route (verified). So (a) the inaccurate ORIGINAL is the only policy artifact, and (b) there is no privacy policy served anywhere in the live app at all. A live consumer product handling personal data with no published privacy policy is itself a CCPA notice-at-collection gap, independent of the content errors — and the header's claim that a corrected version is published is false.
findings.law: CCPA §1798.100(b)/§1798.130(a)(5) + §1798.135(c)(2) (notice at collection / policy must be posted and accessible); FTC Act §5 (failure to provide a posted accurate policy as represented)
evidence: docs/legal/privacy-policy-ORIGINAL.md:4-5 (claims corrected policy + app/privacy/page.tsx exist)
Verified: app/privacy does not exist; docs/legal/ holds only the ORIGINAL
findings.recommended_fix: Produce the corrected policy, publish it at a real route (app/privacy/page.tsx) and link it from join/lobby and host pages so notice-at-collection is satisfied, then make the header's reference true. Until then, do not represent that a corrected/published version exists.

findings.title: Rights table and contact section promise a privacy-request form/route that does not exist (no operational request method)
findings.policy_quote: Privacy request form: [PRIVACY REQUEST FORM URL — build at tr1via.com/privacy-request]
findings.problem: The rights table (right to know/access/delete/correct/portability) repeatedly instructs consumers to 'use the privacy request form,' and the contact block links to tr1via.com/privacy-request — but no such route exists anywhere in app/ (find app -path '*privacy*' returns nothing; there is no app/privacy or app/privacy-request). The CCPA requires a business to provide at least two designated methods for submitting verifiable consumer requests (and at minimum a toll-free number OR, for online-only businesses, an email address plus a web form is the regulatory expectation). Shipping a policy that names a request form that 404s — with a literal '[PRIVACY REQUEST FORM URL — build at ...]' placeholder left in the live text — means California consumers cannot actually exercise access/deletion, and the unedited placeholder is itself evidence the policy was published unfinished. A deletion or access request that has no working intake is a §1798.130 method-of-submission failure, and the dangling placeholder is the kind of obviously-broken artifact that escalates an audit from 'inaccurate' to 'reckless.'
findings.law: Cal. Civ. Code §1798.130(a)(1) (two or more designated methods for submitting requests; email address acceptable for online-only businesses); §1798.105 (right to delete) & §1798.110/§1798.100 (right to know/access) — must be operationally exercisable; 11 CCR §7020 (methods for submitting requests must be functional and easy to execute).
evidence: policy line 218 (literal placeholder '[PRIVACY REQUEST FORM URL — build at tr1via.com/privacy-request]')
policy lines 180-185 ('use the privacy request form' for know/delete/correct/portability)
find app -path '*privacy*' returns nothing; app/privacy and app/privacy-request do not exist
ground-truth: no /privacy-request route and no privacy/legal route anywhere under app/
findings.recommended_fix: Either build a working request intake (a /privacy-request form posting to a real handler, or at minimum honor support@vyntechs.com as the single email method and say so) BEFORE publishing, and delete every 'privacy request form' reference and the placeholder URL. Two methods are not strictly required for an exclusively-online business, but the one method you name must actually work. Remove the literal '[...build at...]' placeholder now.

findings.title: Policy voluntarily asserts CCPA compliance and California-specific rights/timelines while almost certainly below CCPA's business thresholds — creating an enforceable promise it then breaks
findings.policy_quote: For California residents, we will acknowledge within 10 business days and complete within 45 calendar days. If we need more time, we will notify you.
findings.problem: A single-customer, pre-revenue trivia tool run by a Texas sole-DBA almost certainly does NOT meet any of CCPA's three §1798.140(d)(1) business thresholds: (A) $25M+ annual gross revenue, (B) buying/selling/sharing PI of 100,000+ CA consumers/households, or (C) deriving 50%+ of revenue from selling/sharing PI. If TR1VIA is not a 'business' under §1798.140(d), the CCPA does not apply by force of law. BUT the policy voluntarily and specifically claims CCPA applicability — citing CCPA 'sharing,' the 10-business-day acknowledgment / 45-calendar-day completion timeline (§1798.130(a)(2)/11 CCR §7021), right to non-discrimination, GPC honoring, and a CA-specific rights row. Voluntarily adopting a regulatory standard converts it into an enforceable representation under §17200 / FTC-style deception theory: a company is held to the privacy promises it publicly makes regardless of whether the statute independently bound it. So TR1VIA gets the worst of both worlds — it triggers no statutory exemption benefit (it claimed the burden) yet fails to deliver the very things it promised (working GPC, working request form, the 45-day pipeline, accurate retention). The exposure is the gap between promise and practice, not the statute's own reach.
findings.law: Cal. Civ. Code §1798.140(d)(1)(A)-(C) (the three business thresholds — likely none met); §1798.130(a)(2) & 11 CCR §7021 (the 10-day-ack / 45-day-completion timeline the policy adopts and must then honor); Cal. Bus. & Prof. Code §17200 (a voluntarily-made public privacy promise is enforceable as a deceptive practice if broken); FTC Act §5 analog.
evidence: policy lines 186-188, 193 (CA opt-out/non-discrimination rights + 10-business-day/45-calendar-day timeline)
Business context: single paying customer (the first host), pre-scale, Vyntechs DBA — far below 100k CA consumers and $25M revenue (no facts suggesting any threshold met)
Ground-truth: no operational request pipeline, no GPC, no working form to actually meet the 45-day promise
findings.recommended_fix: Decide deliberately: if you do not meet a CCPA business threshold and do not want the obligations, do not claim CCPA-specific machinery — state rights are offered 'as a matter of policy' on a best-efforts basis, drop the precise 10/45-day commitment, and remove unbacked CA mechanisms. If you DO want to claim CCPA compliance (good for trust), you must actually implement the request pipeline, GPC, and accurate notice to meet the promise. Pick one and align the policy to reality; the current half-claimed state is the liability.

findings.title: Cookie/tracking table lists only phantom cookies and omits every cookie the app actually sets — defective notice-at-collection for trackers
findings.policy_quote: | _ga | Google Analytics | Distinguishes unique users | 2 years | ... | CSRF token | TR1VIA | Prevents request forgery | Session |
findings.problem: The cookie table lists _ga, _ga_[ID] (no GA installed), a 'Vercel Analytics' tracker (no @vercel/analytics installed), and a 'CSRF token' cookie (zero csrf/xsrf implementation in the codebase) — all four are phantoms. Meanwhile it omits every cookie the app actually sets: the tr1via_device player cookie (signed UUID, httpOnly, SameSite=lax, Secure in prod, 365-day) and the Supabase sb-* auth-token cookies (host session, @supabase/ssr defaults). Under CCPA the cookie disclosure is part of the at-collection notice of the categories of PI collected and the identifiers used; listing fictional trackers and hiding the real persistent player identifier means a California consumer cannot understand what is actually stored on their device or for how long. The 365-day player identifier is precisely the kind of persistent identifier the consumer has a right to know about, and the table's claim that player session data 'is not retained after a game ends' is contradicted by the very cookie that should be in this table.
findings.law: Cal. Civ. Code §1798.140(v)(1)(A) & (aj) (cookies/device identifiers are PI/unique identifiers requiring disclosure); §1798.100(a) / §1798.130(a)(5)(B) (notice-at-collection of categories collected, including identifiers); §1798.110(c) (right to know categories and specific pieces).
evidence: app/api/session/init/route.ts:25-26,44-53 (tr1via_device — httpOnly, sameSite lax, secure prod, 365d — omitted from table)
middleware.ts:45-60 / lib/supabase/server.ts:48-74 (sb-* auth cookies — omitted)
Repo-wide: no csrf/xsrf code, no @vercel/analytics, no _ga — the four listed cookies are phantoms
policy lines 110-116 (cookie table)
findings.recommended_fix: Replace the cookie table with the real cookies: tr1via_device (player; signed UUID; httpOnly; SameSite=lax; Secure in prod; 365 days; persistent device identifier) and sb-<ref>-auth-token (host session; @supabase/ssr defaults; until logout/expiry). Delete the _ga, _ga_[ID], Vercel Analytics, and CSRF-token rows. The 365-day duration on the player cookie must be stated accurately given the 'not retained after game' claim elsewhere.

findings.title: Host-authentication and Resend email disclosures misstate collection at the point of collection (magic-link email flow does not exist)
findings.policy_quote: When a host account is provisioned, we collect the host's email address to send magic link authentication emails. ... Host email addresses are transmitted to Resend for magic link delivery.
findings.problem: The notice-at-collection for the host's email states a specific purpose and recipient — 'to send magic link authentication emails' and 'transmitted to Resend for magic link delivery' — that the code does not do. There is no email-send library in package.json (no resend/sendgrid/postmark/nodemailer), and host login at /login posts the email to /api/auth/founder-login which mints a Supabase session server-side via generateLink + verifyOtp with no email round-trip; the login page itself states 'No magic link, no OTP, no email round-trip.' The only magic-link-style URL (admin grant) is generated and handed off manually by the founder, never emailed. Under CCPA §1798.100(a)/(b), the at-collection notice must accurately state the purpose for which each category of PI is collected; stating the host email is collected to transmit to Resend for email delivery — a transmission that never occurs — misdescribes both the purpose and the recipient. This is a notice-accuracy defect (lower CCPA stakes than the player-facing items because it concerns a tiny host population and no false data flow actually leaks, but it is still a false collection-purpose statement and it names a processor in the sharing table that receives nothing).
findings.law: Cal. Civ. Code §1798.100(a) & (b) (notice at or before collection of the purposes for which categories of PI are collected/used — must be accurate); §1798.130(a)(5)(B)-(C) (categories collected and third parties disclosed-to); §1798.140(ag) (service-provider designation — Resend is not one here).
evidence: package.json dependencies — no resend/sendgrid/postmark/nodemailer (no send path)
app/api/auth/founder-login/route.ts:70-101 (server-side generateLink+verifyOtp; no email sent)
app/(host)/login/page.tsx:1-10 ('No magic link, no OTP, no email round-trip')
app/api/admin/grant-magic-link/route.ts:97-119 (grant URL returned for manual handoff, not emailed)
policy lines 96-98, 100-102, 151 (Resend/magic-link claims)
findings.recommended_fix: Correct the host section to the real flow: 'Hosts sign in by entering their email at the login page; the server verifies the email against the host list and creates a session directly — no email is sent. Some hosts are onboarded via a one-time sign-in link the operator shares directly.' Remove the Resend sub-processor row and the 'transmitted to Resend' sentence. Keep host email disclosed as collected for account identification/authentication.

findings.title: No mechanism to act on "actual knowledge" of a child user — disclaimer promise to "delete promptly" is undeliverable
findings.policy_quote: If you are a parent or guardian and believe your child under 13 has submitted information through TR1VIA, contact us at support@vyntechs.com and we will delete it promptly.
findings.problem: The COPPA "actual knowledge" standard (16 C.F.R. §312.2/§312.3) attaches the operator's full obligations the moment it learns a specific user is under 13 — and the policy literally invites parents to provide that knowledge. But the ground truth shows there is NO deletion endpoint or admin tooling for player data anywhere in the codebase: the only deletion routine (`reset_night_to_setup`) explicitly PRESERVES players and never deletes display names, and even that is host-initiated, not founder/support-initiated. So when a parent emails support@vyntechs.com, the operator now has actual knowledge of a sub-13 child but no technical way to honor the "delete it promptly" promise. That converts a disclaimer into an affirmative, unkept commitment — both a COPPA compliance gap (failure to delete PI of a known child) and an FTC "unfair/deceptive practice" exposure (Section 5) for promising deletion the system can't perform. Because the data (device_id, display_name, answers, ms_to_lock timing, IP-linked logs) persists indefinitely, the child's PI remains live and re-identifiable across every future night that device joins.
findings.law: 16 C.F.R. §312.2 ("actual knowledge"), §312.5 / §312.6 (parental right to review and delete), §312.10 (data retention/deletion); FTC Act §5, 15 U.S.C. §45 (deceptive failure to honor stated deletion commitment); cf. FTC v. Epic Games (2022) — failure to delete known child data
evidence: GROUND TRUTH: no deletion endpoint or admin tooling for player data; only reset_night_to_setup exists and it counts players as KEPT
supabase/migrations/0009_reset_night_to_setup_adjustments.sql:71-95 (reset preserves players, only wipes answers/reveals for live/done games)
app/api/nights/[id]/close/route.ts:26-30 (closing a night stamps closed_at only; deletes nothing)
docs/legal/privacy-policy-ORIGINAL.md:218 (privacy request form URL is still a [PLACEHOLDER], so there is no working intake channel either)
findings.recommended_fix: Build a real child-data deletion path before relying on the disclaimer: a support-triggered admin action that, given a date+venue+display name (or device hint), deletes the matching players row(s), answers, game_participations, game_scores, adjustments, and scrubs IP from logs where feasible, and confirms back to the parent. Document an internal SLA (e.g., 10 business days). Until that exists, do not promise "delete it promptly" — that is currently deceptive.

findings.title: "Not directed to children" / general-audience position is weak given family-venue deployment and frictionless anyone-can-join design
findings.policy_quote: TR1VIA is used in bars, restaurants, and venues that may admit minors, but the platform itself is not designed for, marketed to, or intended for children.
findings.problem: COPPA's "directed to children" analysis (16 C.F.R. §312.2) is a multi-factor totality test, not a self-declaration — it weighs subject matter, visual/audio content, animated characters, use of child-oriented activities/incentives (games), AND "empirical evidence regarding audience composition" and "evidence regarding the intended audience." TR1VIA is a brightly-themed game (the codebase shows "Endless Evening" theming, animated water/ceremony effects, chimes, gold-glow stingers — child-appealing presentation) deliberately deployed in family venues like pizza places where the operator's OWN policy concedes minors are present, accessible to anyone with no account and no friction. The FTC has rejected the bar/restaurant-context defense before: an interactive game that any present child can join, that is visually playful, risks being deemed at least a "mixed audience" service. For a mixed-audience service the FTC's 2025 COPPA amendments and existing guidance require an age screen and child-directed-level protections for users who indicate they are under 13 — the disclaimer alone is not a safe harbor. the first host's go-live at Soul Fire Pizza (a pizza venue per project memory) is precisely the high-risk fact pattern.
findings.law: 16 C.F.R. §312.2 (multi-factor "directed to children" / "mixed audience" definition, as amended eff. 2025); FTC COPPA FAQ §D (mixed audience, neutral age screen); FTC v. HyperBeard (2020) — playful game deemed child-directed despite operator's general-audience claim
evidence: docs/legal/privacy-policy-ORIGINAL.md:28 (operator concedes use in venues that admit minors)
components/player/PlayerJoin.tsx + components/player/PlayerLobby.tsx (frictionless join: 6-char code + nickname, no account, no age step)
lib/theme/lockInCeremony.ts; components/system/TVTimerArc.tsx (animated, playful, game-show presentation — relevant to child-appeal factors)
MEMORY: first live show at Soul Fire Pizza (family/pizza venue)
findings.recommended_fix: Treat TR1VIA as at least a mixed-audience service. Add a neutral age screen at join (no pre-filled value; remembered for the session). For any user who indicates under 13, do not collect persistent identifiers, do not store the display name, and run an ephemeral session — or block play. Keep the disclaimer but stop treating it as the whole compliance posture. Have counsel confirm the directed-to-children factor analysis before the next family-venue deployment.

findings.title: Free-text display name (1–40 chars, no filter) invites a child to type real identifying info; stored and broadcast with no minimization
findings.policy_quote: If you enter a nickname or display name to join a game, that name is visible to others in the session and to the host.
findings.problem: The display-name field is `z.string().trim().min(1).max(40)` with zero content validation (lib/api/schemas.ts:71). A child will routinely type a real first name + last initial, a full name, a school, or a sports-team-plus-number — all child PI under COPPA when combined with the persistent device_id and IP the system already holds. That free-text PI is then upserted to Postgres (persists indefinitely), shown on the TV and to the host, and broadcast over Supabase Realtime (broadcastPlayerJoined). COPPA's data-minimization principle (§312.7) bars conditioning participation on more PI than reasonably necessary, and the FTC has flagged free-text fields that capture children's real names. Pairing an unfiltered real-name field with a 365-day identifier and indefinite retention is the opposite of minimization. The policy also falsely claims these names are "deleted when game session ends," so a parent has no accurate notice of what actually persists.
findings.law: 16 C.F.R. §312.2 (first/last name as PI; combination with persistent identifier), §312.7 (prohibition on conditioning participation on unnecessary collection / data minimization); FTC v. Musical.ly (2019) — collection of children's names/profiles
evidence: lib/api/schemas.ts:69-72 (CreatePlayerSchema.displayName: z.string().trim().min(1).max(40) — no profanity/PII/age filter)
app/api/players/route.ts:62-80 (display_name upserted to Postgres, persists; broadcast to TV/host)
docs/legal/privacy-policy-ORIGINAL.md:63 ("We do not retain player display names after a game ends" — false per ground truth; names persist indefinitely)
GROUND TRUTH: player display names persist indefinitely; host dashboard reads them from CLOSED nights
findings.recommended_fix: Add inline guidance/placeholder steering players to a nickname ("Pick a fun nickname — don't use your real name"), and consider light validation to discourage full real names. Make the retention statement TRUE (names persist) or actually delete names on game end. For users who indicate under 13 (per the age screen), do not store the typed name at all — use a generated handle. This both reduces child-PI exposure and fixes the false retention claim.

findings.title: No under-16 opt-in for sale/share and no working GPC — CCPA minors machinery is asserted but absent
findings.policy_quote: Enabling a Global Privacy Control (GPC) signal in your browser — TR1VIA honors GPC signals as opt-out requests
findings.problem: California's CCPA/CPRA (Cal. Civ. Code §1798.120(c)) flips the default for minors: a business may NOT sell or share the personal information of a consumer it has actual knowledge is under 16 without affirmative opt-IN (opt-in by a parent for under-13, by the minor for 13–15). The policy itself states GA4 transmission "may constitute sharing under CCPA" — yet (a) the ground truth shows GPC is not implemented at all (no Sec-GPC / navigator.globalPrivacyControl read anywhere), making "TR1VIA honors GPC signals" an affirmative false statement the California AG has specifically penalized (Sephora 2022, $1.2M, for misrepresenting GPC handling), and (b) there is no under-16 opt-in gate, so if any "sharing" did occur it would be unlawful by default for the minors who demonstrably use the product. Several other states (e.g., Connecticut CTDPA, Colorado CPA) also require opt-in consent to process/sell minors' data or to target them. The minor-specific layer is entirely unaddressed beyond boilerplate.
findings.law: Cal. Civ. Code §1798.120(c) (no sale/share of under-16 PI without opt-in) & §1798.135 (GPC honoring); Cal. AG settlement In re Sephora (2022, $1.2M — misrepresented GPC); Connecticut Data Privacy Act §6(a)(5) (opt-in for minors' targeted ads/sale, eff. 2024 amendments); Colorado Privacy Act minors provisions (SB 24-041)
evidence: GROUND TRUTH: no code reads Sec-GPC or navigator.globalPrivacyControl anywhere; "TR1VIA honors GPC" is unimplemented (affirmative false statement)
middleware.ts:29-86 (request handling — no GPC signal read)
docs/legal/privacy-policy-ORIGINAL.md:86,92,202 (CCPA "sharing" + GPC claims)
GROUND TRUTH: GA4 is a phantom — but the minors opt-in obligation attaches to ANY future analytics/share, and the policy already asserts sharing may occur
findings.recommended_fix: Either implement GPC honoring (read Sec-GPC header / navigator.globalPrivacyControl and actually change behavior) or delete every claim that TR1VIA honors GPC — the false claim alone is the Sephora liability. If any sale/share is ever wired in, gate it behind under-16 opt-in (parental for under-13). Given GA4 is currently a phantom, the cleanest fix is to remove the entire CCPA-sharing/GA4/GPC narrative until a real analytics flow with real minors-handling exists.

findings.title: No EU/UK Article 27 representative appointed (and none in code/operations), despite globally reachable site processing EU/UK personal data
findings.policy_quote: If TR1VIA expands to serve EU or UK customers, this policy will be updated to include an EU/UK representative before that expansion takes place.
findings.problem: Art. 27 GDPR (and the standalone UK Art. 27) require a controller with no EU/UK establishment that is subject to Art. 3(2) to designate, in writing, a representative IN the Union (and separately in the UK) — and to publish that representative's identity so data subjects and supervisory authorities can contact it. TR1VIA names none. The policy promises to add one 'before expansion,' but as established above, scope can attach on visitor processing, not on a marketing decision the controller makes later. The Art. 27(2) exemptions are narrow (occasional processing, no large-scale special-category, low risk) and almost certainly unavailable here: processing is continuous (every page load, every game), involves persistent online identifiers (365-day device cookie) and IP-geolocation, so it is neither 'occasional' nor plainly low-risk. Absence of a representative is independently fineable and has been actively enforced.
findings.law: GDPR Art. 27 (EU representative) and UK GDPR Art. 27 (separate UK representative required post-Brexit); Art. 27(2) exemption criteria; ICO has issued reprimands/enforcement for missing UK representatives; supervisory authorities treat Art. 27 as a standalone infringement under Art. 83(4)(a)
evidence: docs/legal/privacy-policy-ORIGINAL.md:161 (representative deferred to future expansion)
grep app/ components/ lib/ middleware.ts for 'representative'/'Art 27'/'DPO': zero matches (not implemented or named anywhere)
app/api/session/init/route.ts:47-53 (continuous, identifier-based processing — undercuts any Art. 27(2) 'occasional' exemption)
findings.recommended_fix: If EU/UK subjects are in scope, designate a written EU representative AND a UK representative and publish each entity's name + contact in the policy. If relying on the Art. 27(2) exemption, document the occasional/low-risk analysis (it likely fails given the persistent device cookie). Otherwise, hard geo-block EU/UK and say so.

findings.title: DPA-with-processor claims ("confirmed or are confirming DPAs with Vercel and Google") are unverifiable and cover a processor that isn't used
findings.policy_quote: Each provider acts as a data processor — processing data on our behalf, bound by data processing agreements (DPAs). We have confirmed or are confirming DPAs with Vercel and Google.
findings.problem: Art. 28(3) GDPR requires a written controller-processor contract (DPA) with binding terms for every processor of EU/UK personal data. The policy asserts DPAs with 'Vercel and Google,' but (a) Google/GA4 is not wired into the codebase at all, so a DPA 'with Google' protects no real data flow and signals the disclosure was drafted to a fictional architecture; (b) it omits the processors that actually handle EU/UK personal data — Supabase (display names, answers, device-id, player IPs via Realtime) and Pexels (viewer IP/User-Agent via direct CDN loads) — so no Art. 28 coverage is even claimed for the genuine processors. 'Confirmed or are confirming' is also a hedge that, if untrue when an EU/UK subject is processed, is a direct Art. 28(1) breach (controller must only use processors providing sufficient guarantees, under contract). This is medium rather than high because the Art. 28 gap is curable by paperwork and the immediate fine exposure is smaller than the missing-representative/transfer gaps — but it is still a real GDPR defect.
findings.law: GDPR Art. 28(1)-(3) (processor contracts + sufficient-guarantees obligation); UK GDPR Art. 28 equivalent
evidence: docs/legal/privacy-policy-ORIGINAL.md:153 (DPA claim limited to Vercel + Google)
package.json:13-23 (no GA dependency — 'Google' is not a processor in this codebase)
lib/supabase/admin.ts:1-33 (Supabase service-role client over user data — a real processor, not DPA-named)
lib/pexels/search.ts:14-18 + components/player/PlayerQuestion.tsx:158-159 (Pexels receives query text + viewer IP — real processor, not DPA-named)
findings.recommended_fix: Execute and reference Art. 28(3) DPAs with the REAL processors (Vercel, Supabase, Pexels, Anthropic). Remove the Google DPA reference. Replace 'confirmed or are confirming' with a definite, accurate statement; do not assert a DPA exists until it does.

findings.title: Legal-basis table omits the consent basis the cookies/analytics actually require and mislabels analytics as legitimate interest
findings.policy_quote: Analytics and performance | Legitimate interest | Disclosed use
findings.problem: Art. 6 GDPR requires the correct lawful basis per purpose, and Art. 13(1)(c)/(d) requires the policy to state it (and the legitimate-interest pursued, where that basis is used). Two problems for EU/UK subjects: (1) Where ePrivacy/PECR requires consent to set a non-essential cookie (the 365-day device identifier; any analytics), the GDPR basis for the subsequent processing is generally CONSENT, not legitimate interest — the EDPB (Opinion 5/2019 on ePrivacy/GDPR interplay) is clear you cannot launder a consent-required cookie into a 'legitimate interest' GDPR basis. The table lists 'Analytics and performance | Legitimate interest,' which is the wrong basis for cookie-based analytics in the EU/UK and includes no legitimate-interest balancing reference. (2) The table assigns bases to analytics that doesn't exist in the code, so the basis is asserted for a phantom purpose while the real consent-requiring identifier (device cookie) gets no basis row at all. The result is an inaccurate Art. 6 mapping that would not survive a DPA's legal-basis assessment. Medium because it is curable by rewriting the table and is downstream of the consent/retention defects, but it is a genuine GDPR transparency gap.
findings.law: GDPR Art. 6(1) (lawful basis), Art. 13(1)(c)-(d) (state basis + legitimate interests pursued); EDPB Opinion 5/2019 on the interplay of ePrivacy Directive and GDPR (consent-required processing cannot fall back on legitimate interest)
evidence: docs/legal/privacy-policy-ORIGINAL.md:120-128 (legal-basis table — analytics as legitimate interest; no consent row; no LI balancing)
app/api/session/init/route.ts:47-53 (365-day device identifier — a consent-requiring processing with no basis row in the table)
package.json:13-23 (analytics not installed — basis asserted for a non-existent purpose)
findings.recommended_fix: Rebuild the Art. 6 table against real processing: state the actual basis for the device cookie (consent for EU/UK if non-essential, or strict-necessity if scoped down), set analytics to consent if/when installed, and add a legitimate-interest balancing summary where LI is genuinely relied on (e.g., security).

findings.title: Breach-notification clause misapplies the GDPR 72-hour rule to host notification and silently drops the supervisory-authority and player duties
findings.policy_quote: If a security breach occurs that affects hosts' personal data, we will notify affected hosts within 72 hours of becoming aware of the breach, as required by applicable law.
findings.problem: The 72-hour deadline in GDPR Art. 33 is the controller's duty to notify the SUPERVISORY AUTHORITY, not data subjects. Notification to affected data subjects under Art. 34 has a different trigger ('without undue delay' when there is high risk), not a flat 72-hour rule. The policy (a) invents a 72-hour host-notification SLA that GDPR does not impose and that the controller may not be able to meet, then labels it 'as required by applicable law' — a misstatement of the legal duty; (b) limits breach notification to HOSTS only, ignoring PLAYERS, whose display names + answers + device-id are personal data that could be exposed in the same breach (and whose data, per the retention findings, is retained indefinitely and thus more exposed); and (c) omits the Art. 33 supervisory-authority notification entirely. For EU/UK subjects this both over-commits (a 72h subject-notification SLA the controller has bound itself to) and under-discloses (no SA notice, no player notice). Medium because it is a disclosure/contractual-promise defect rather than an immediate large fine, but the false 'as required by applicable law' framing is the kind of misstatement a regulator notes.
findings.law: GDPR Art. 33 (72h notification to supervisory authority) and Art. 34 (notification to data subjects without undue delay, high-risk trigger); UK GDPR Arts. 33-34 (notify ICO)
evidence: docs/legal/privacy-policy-ORIGINAL.md:172 (72h applied to host notification, labeled 'as required by applicable law'; players omitted)
supabase/migrations/0001_init.sql:113-122 (player display_name + device_id are personal data exposable in a breach)
app/api/nights/[id]/close/route.ts:26-30 + app/host/page.tsx:72-77 (player data retained indefinitely — higher breach exposure than the host-only clause admits)
findings.recommended_fix: Separate the duties: state the Art. 33 supervisory-authority notification (72h to the relevant DPA/ICO), state Art. 34 data-subject notification ('without undue delay' on high risk) covering BOTH hosts and players, and stop labeling a self-imposed 72h host SLA as a legal requirement.

findings.title: Texas TDPSA (their home state) applicability and notice-content adequacy not squarely addressed
findings.policy_quote: Right to appeal | Virginia, Colorado, Texas | Email support@vyntechs.com within 30 days of our decision
findings.problem: TR1VIA is a Texas business processing Texas residents' data, so the Texas Data Privacy and Security Act (TDPSA, effective July 1, 2024) is the most likely state law to be enforced against them — and the Texas AG has been actively enforcing it. The TDPSA has NO revenue/volume threshold; it applies to any entity that conducts business in Texas and processes personal data, exempting only businesses that qualify as 'small businesses' under the SBA — and even small businesses must obtain consent before selling sensitive data. The policy mentions Texas only once (the appeal row) and never (a) identifies whether TR1VIA claims the small-business exemption, (b) provides the TDPSA-mandated notice content, or (c) includes the TDPSA's specific required sentence where applicable. The TDPSA also uniquely requires a conspicuous 'We may sell your sensitive/biometric personal data' notice IF that occurs (it doesn't here, so it can be omitted, but the determination must be made), and requires honoring opt-out preference signals (GPC) for sales/targeted-ads by Jan 1, 2025 — which ties directly to the false 'TR1VIA honors GPC' statement (no GPC code exists). The appeal mechanism (30 days) is named but the TDPSA's full consumer-rights set and the controller's 45-day response window (extendable 45 more) and the right to escalate a denied appeal to the Texas AG are not laid out.
findings.law: Tex. Bus. & Com. Code ch. 541 (TDPSA): § 541.002 (applicability — no revenue threshold; SBA small-business carve-out), § 541.051-.052 (consumer rights + 45-day response, 45-day extension), § 541.055 (appeal process + AG-complaint link), § 541.102 (privacy-notice content), § 541.055/§ 541.0555 (universal opt-out / GPC honoring by Jan 1, 2025). Texas AG TDPSA enforcement actions (2024-2025).
evidence: policy line 188: Texas appears only in the appeal row; no TDPSA notice-content section

policy line 92, 202: 'TR1VIA honors GPC signals' — TDPSA requires honoring opt-out signals for sales/targeted ads, and GROUND TRUTH confirms zero GPC code exists, so this is a TDPSA-relevant false statement
MEMORY: business is Cleburne, TX (Johnson County) — Texas is the home forum and TDPSA the primary applicable state act
GROUND TRUTH: controller = Vyntechs (DBA), holds hosts.email + player display names + device-id — personal data triggering TDPSA
findings.recommended_fix: Add a Texas-specific section: state the consumer rights set, the 45-day (+45) response window, the appeal process, and that a denied appeal may be submitted to the Texas Attorney General (with the AG complaint link). Make the express applicability determination (small-business exemption or not) and, if not selling sensitive data, simply omit the sensitive-data sale notice. Critically, only retain the GPC-honoring language AFTER GPC is actually implemented in middleware — otherwise delete it (it's a false statement under the very law most likely to be enforced here).

findings.title: Anthropic generates trivia questions from host-entered prompt text; undisclosed sub-processor
findings.policy_quote: MISSING
findings.problem: Anthropic (@anthropic-ai/sdk) receives host-supplied topic/flavor/difficulty prompt text plus a static system prompt to generate questions — a US third party processing host-entered content. It receives no player or host personal data (no emails, names, IPs), so risk is lower than Supabase/Pexels, but the policy's blanket 'We do not share your data with third parties except as described below' is still incomplete: a sub-processor receiving host-entered content is undisclosed. Low severity because no personal data flows, but it should be listed for completeness and to make the 'except as described' sentence true.
findings.law: CCPA §1798.130(a)(5) (third-party disclosure completeness); FTC Act §5 (15 U.S.C. §45 — completeness of sharing disclosure)
evidence: lib/ai/generate-questions.ts:42-46,180-184,216-269 (Anthropic client; sends prompt text, no PII)
package.json: @anthropic-ai/sdk present
findings.recommended_fix: Add a row: 'Anthropic (US) — AI processor — receives the trivia topic/instructions a host types to generate questions; no player or host personal data is sent.'

findings.title: 'We have confirmed or are confirming DPAs with Vercel and Google' — vague hedge plus a DPA with a phantom processor
findings.policy_quote: Each provider acts as a data processor — processing data on our behalf, bound by data processing agreements (DPAs). We have confirmed or are confirming DPAs with Vercel and Google.
findings.problem: Two problems. (1) 'confirmed OR are confirming' is a weasel hedge that asserts the protective fact (data is 'bound by DPAs') while admitting it may not be true yet — if a DPA is not actually in place, the flat statement that each provider is 'bound by data processing agreements' is a false assurance of contractual safeguards. (2) The named DPAs are with Vercel and Google, but Google (GA4) is a phantom processor and the actual processors holding personal data (Supabase, Pexels) are unnamed and presumably uncovered by this sentence. So the assurance covers the wrong parties. Stating contractual safeguards exist when they may not, and tying them to a non-existent processor, is a deceptive assurance.
findings.law: FTC Act §5 (15 U.S.C. §45 — false assurance of safeguards); GDPR Art. 28 (processor DPAs required, relevant on stated EU expansion); CCPA §1798.140(ag)/(ai) + §1798.100(d) (service-provider contracts required)
evidence: docs/legal/privacy-policy-ORIGINAL.md:153 (the hedged DPA sentence)
Google/GA4 is a phantom processor (no GA installed — verified)
Supabase/Pexels are the real processors and are unnamed
findings.recommended_fix: State only what is true. After fixing the processor list to Supabase/Vercel/Pexels/Anthropic, write: 'We have data processing agreements in place with [the processors that actually have signed DPAs].' Do not assert 'bound by DPAs' for any processor without a signed DPA, and remove the Google reference.

findings.title: Policy states COPPA compliance for a product used in venues that admit minors, with no age gate or age-data controls in code
findings.policy_quote: We comply with the Children's Online Privacy Protection Act (COPPA).
findings.problem: The policy flatly asserts COPPA compliance and acknowledges the platform is used in venues that may admit minors, yet there is no age gate, no neutral age screen, and no special handling of under-13 data anywhere in the code — and a free-text display name from a child would be stored and persisted like any other (no deletion mechanism, per the deletion findings). A blanket 'we comply with COPPA' with no implementing controls is an overstated compliance claim; COPPA exposure here is genuinely low (not child-directed), but the unqualified compliance assertion is the kind of statement the FTC scrutinizes when controls are absent. Low severity given the not-directed-at-children posture, but the flat claim should be softened.
findings.law: Children's Online Privacy Protection Act, 15 U.S.C. §6501-6506 + 16 CFR Part 312; FTC Act §5 (overstated compliance claim)
evidence: docs/legal/privacy-policy-ORIGINAL.md:24-30 (COPPA compliance claim; venues admit minors)
Verified: no age gate / under-13 handling in app; no deletion path for any player data
findings.recommended_fix: Soften to the accurate posture: 'TR1VIA is not directed to children under 13 and we do not knowingly collect their personal information; if we learn we have, we will delete it.' Drop the unqualified 'we comply with COPPA' unless implementing controls (and a working deletion path) are in place.

findings.title: Host/venue is positioned as the de facto gatekeeper but cannot supply verifiable parental consent for children who join
findings.policy_quote: Hosts — venue staff or trivia organizers who log in to run games.
findings.problem: The design implicitly relies on the venue/host to control who plays, but a bar/restaurant host is not the parent and cannot give COPPA-verifiable parental consent (16 C.F.R. §312.5) on a child's behalf. COPPA's "school/operator" consent-by-proxy exception is narrow and applies to ed-tech in schools for educational purposes — it does not extend to a commercial trivia game in a restaurant. So there is no lawful consent path for any sub-13 child who joins: the parent isn't in the loop, the host can't consent, and the platform collects PI by default. This compounds the other findings (it's why the disclaimer carries all the weight). Lower severity standalone because it's a structural consequence rather than a separate violation, but it should inform the chosen remedy (the only safe paths are: don't collect PI from under-13s, or obtain real verifiable parental consent — the venue can't shortcut either).
findings.law: 16 C.F.R. §312.5 (verifiable parental consent — required methods); FTC COPPA FAQ §N (school consent exception limited to educational context, not commercial)
evidence: docs/legal/privacy-policy-ORIGINAL.md:22 (hosts are venue staff, not parents)
components/player/PlayerJoin.tsx (any present person, including a child, joins directly; host does not vet age)
GROUND TRUTH: no consent flow of any kind exists in code
findings.recommended_fix: Do not lean on the host/venue as a consent proxy — it is not legally sufficient. Choose a path that does not require parental consent at all: a neutral age screen that routes under-13 users into a no-PI ephemeral mode (no device cookie, no stored name, IP not persisted). That is the only realistic compliance route for a walk-up restaurant game; obtaining verifiable parental consent at a pizza counter is impractical.

findings.title: Controller identity is a DBA with no legal entity, no DPO/representative contact, and a single shared support email — insufficient Art. 13 identification for EU/UK subjects
findings.policy_quote: TR1VIA is operated by Vyntechs (DBA), based in Texas, United States. Our mailing address is 1712 Raylene Dr, Cleburne, TX 76033. Questions: support@vyntechs.com
findings.problem: Art. 13(1)(a)-(b) requires the policy to give the controller's identity and contact details, and (where applicable) the DPO's and the Art. 27 representative's contact details. The policy identifies the controller only as 'Vyntechs (DBA)' — a doing-business-as, not a named legal entity (LLC/Inc.) — with a residential mailing address and a single shared support@ inbox that doubles as the privacy-rights channel, the security-report channel, and general questions. For a US-only audience this is thin but tolerable; for EU/UK subjects it falls short: no identifiable legal person to hold accountable, no DPO contact (Art. 37 assessment not evidenced), and no Art. 27 representative contact (covered separately). It also undermines the rights mechanism, since the only working channel is an email that the code shows leads to no /privacy-request route and no deletion tooling. Low severity on its own — it is a disclosure-completeness gap rather than a fine driver — but it compounds the representative and rights-mechanism findings.
findings.law: GDPR Art. 13(1)(a)-(b) (controller identity + contact, DPO contact where applicable), Art. 37 (DPO designation assessment); UK GDPR equivalents
evidence: docs/legal/privacy-policy-ORIGINAL.md:10,15 ('Vyntechs (DBA)' — no registered legal entity named; residential address; single support@ inbox)
docs/legal/privacy-policy-ORIGINAL.md:218 (privacy request form URL is an unbuilt placeholder)
grep app/ for /privacy-request or privacy route: none exists — the only rights channel is the shared email
findings.recommended_fix: State the full registered legal entity behind the DBA, give a dedicated privacy contact (and DPO/EU+UK representative if in scope), and either build the /privacy-request form or remove the reference so the only rights channel that works is honestly described.

findings.title: No special-category data processed — the policy is correct by omission, but should affirmatively state it for EU/UK clarity
findings.policy_quote: MISSING
findings.problem: Assessed for completeness within the GDPR lens: the code processes display names, answer choices/timing, device-id, IP, and host email — none of which are Art. 9 special categories, and the AI/topic flow sends no PII to Anthropic. So the policy does NOT wrongly process special-category data, and its silence is not a false statement. However, the policy never affirmatively states 'we do not process special-category data,' which is the conventional EU/UK transparency assurance and would close the question for a supervisory authority. This is a genuine-low, near-informational item: there is no real exposure (no Art. 9 processing exists), but adding the explicit statement is cheap and expected. I flag it so the absence is on the record as assessed-and-clear rather than overlooked. The only adjacent risk worth noting is age: the COPPA section concedes venues may admit minors, and if an under-16 EU child plays, a display name could implicate Art. 8 (child consent / information-society services) — but that is an age-of-consent edge, not special-category processing.
findings.law: GDPR Art. 9 (special categories — none processed here); Art. 8 (children's consent for information-society services, EU member-state age 13-16) flagged only as an adjacent edge
evidence: supabase/migrations/0001_init.sql:113-153 (player data = display_name, answers, timing, device_id — no Art. 9 categories)
lib/ai/generate-questions.ts:42-46,180-184 (Anthropic receives topic prompt text only — no PII, no special-category data)
docs/legal/privacy-policy-ORIGINAL.md:24-30 (COPPA section concedes minors may be present — Art. 8 edge, not Art. 9)
findings.recommended_fix: Add an explicit 'We do not process special-category (Art. 9) data' line for EU/UK clarity. Separately, if EU children may play, address Art. 8 child-consent age thresholds rather than relying solely on COPPA's US under-13 framing.

findings.title: Virginia/Colorado/Connecticut/Utah obligations partially covered — appeal right and rights set incomplete for CT/UT
findings.policy_quote: Right to appeal | Virginia, Colorado, Texas | Email support@vyntechs.com within 30 days of our decision
findings.problem: The appeal row lists Virginia, Colorado, and Texas but omits Connecticut, which ALSO grants a statutory right to appeal a denied request (CTDPA). Utah's UCPA does not grant an appeal right, so omitting Utah there is correct — but the policy never addresses Utah's distinct rights set at all, and never names Connecticut as a covered state anywhere. More importantly, the consumer-facing appeal mechanic is stated backwards-incomplete: under VCDPA/CPA/CTDPA the controller must respond to the appeal within 60 days (VA) / 45 days (CO/CT) and must provide an online mechanism (or other means) to contact the relevant state AG if the appeal is denied — none of which is disclosed. The '30 days of our decision' window the policy gives the consumer to appeal is also not the operative deadline in these statutes (they don't impose a consumer 30-day appeal-filing cap the way the policy implies). Whether these laws even apply turns on processing thresholds (e.g., VCDPA: 100k consumers, or 25k + 50% revenue from sale) that a single-customer live-trivia app almost certainly does not meet — so the cleanest fix may be to scope the multi-state rights honestly rather than over-promise obligations TR1VIA isn't subject to.
findings.law: Va. Code § 59.1-577(C) (VCDPA appeal, 60-day controller response + AG-contact mechanism); Colo. Rev. Stat. § 6-1-1306(3) (CPA appeal, 45 days); Conn. Gen. Stat. § 42-520 (CTDPA appeal, 60 days + AG complaint link) — Connecticut OMITTED from policy; Utah Code § 13-61-101 et seq. (UCPA — no appeal right, narrower rights set).
evidence: policy line 188: appeal row lists 'Virginia, Colorado, Texas' — Connecticut missing
policy lines 178-190: rights table is generic ('Everyone'/'EU/UK'/'California') and never names Connecticut or Utah specifically
policy line 188: consumer '30 days of our decision' framing does not match the statutory controller-response windows or the required AG-escalation mechanism
GROUND TRUTH: single paying customer (the first host) — processing volumes are far below VCDPA/CPA/CTDPA applicability thresholds; over-promising these obligations creates avoidable exposure
findings.recommended_fix: Either (a) add Connecticut to the appeal row and add a short paragraph stating, for each appeal state, that TR1VIA will respond to appeals within the statutory window and provide a method to contact that state's Attorney General if denied; or (b) given the tiny processing footprint, state plainly that TR1VIA may not meet the applicability thresholds of these comprehensive state laws but will honor access/deletion/correction requests from any resident as a matter of policy, and route appeals to support@vyntechs.com with AG-escalation noted. Remove the misleading '30 days of our decision' consumer cap. Do not claim Utah grants an appeal right.

findings.title: Self-imposed 72-hour breach-notification promise exceeds US-law requirements and manufactures contractual liability
findings.policy_quote: If a security breach occurs that affects hosts' personal data, we will notify affected hosts within 72 hours of becoming aware of the breach, as required by applicable law.
findings.problem: No US state breach-notification statute imposes a flat 72-hour deadline (that figure is GDPR's regulator-notice rule, which doesn't apply to a US-only venue business). Texas Bus. & Com. Code § 521.053 requires notice 'without unreasonable delay and not later than 60 days.' By promising 72 hours 'as required by applicable law,' the policy (a) misstates the applicable law and (b) converts a self-imposed deadline into a contractual/§5-FTC commitment the operator will almost certainly miss in a real incident (a solo operator cannot scope a breach, identify affected hosts, and notify in 72 hours) — turning a security incident into a SECOND, independent breach-of-promise claim. This is the classic 'the promise, not the breach, is what gets you sued' trap.
findings.law: Tex. Bus. & Com. Code § 521.053 (notice without unreasonable delay, ≤ 60 days; AG notice if ≥250 Texans); Cal. Civ. Code § 1798.82 (most expedient time / without unreasonable delay); FTC Act § 5 (failure to honor a published security promise is deceptive). 72-hour figure derives from GDPR Art. 33, inapplicable to US-only ops.
evidence: policy line 172: 'notify affected hosts within 72 hours of becoming aware of the breach, as required by applicable law' — no US statute requires 72 hours; misstates 'applicable law'
GROUND TRUTH: solo operator (Brandon), no email-send library in the app at all — there is literally no built mechanism to mass-notify hosts within 72 hours
policy line 22 + GROUND TRUTH: host auth has no email-send path; breach-notice delivery capability is unproven
findings.recommended_fix: Change to: 'we will notify affected hosts without unreasonable delay and within the time required by applicable law (in Texas, no later than 60 days).' Do not state a self-imposed 72-hour deadline. Ensure an actual notification channel exists (the app currently has no email-send library), or the promise is unkeepable regardless of the deadline.
